sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 26, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves active green frame right by 3 cols VERIFIED', 'LEFT': 'Moves active green frame left by 3 cols VERIFIED', 'UP': 'Moves active green frame up by 3 rows VERIFIED', 'DOWN': 'Moves active green frame down by 3 rows VERIFIED', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking on green frame selects it (gives white center), clicking on center pixel changes its color VERIFIED'}
- Object taxonomy: {'g': 'gray background', 'w': 'white panels/squares', 'p': 'purple vertical bar (barrier between panels)', 'N': 'light green 3x3 frames (movable tokens)', 'c': 'charcoal 5x5 frames (targets)', 'W': 'white single pixel (selected token center)', 'B': 'black single pixel (unselected token center)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'row 63, charcoal strip'}
- Win pattern: {'rule': 'Move each green frame into a charcoal target frame. Select frame with MOUSE, then use arrow keys to move it.'}
Current level:
- World model: Right frame active (white center). Currently at rows 30-32, cols 27-29. Just executed UP+6xRIGHT. Need to check if frame reached right target at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. Left frame at rows 30-32, cols 18-20 with charcoal center (not active). Left target at rows 32-36, cols 11-15.
- Goal model: Move green frames into charcoal target frames. Right frame needs to go to right target (rows 26-30, cols 44-48). Left frame needs to go to left target (rows 32-36, cols 11-15).
- Recent findings: ['MOUSE selects active frame (gives white center)', 'Arrow keys move active frame by 3 pixels', 'RIGHT frame moved right with RIGHT key', 'UP moves frame up by 3 rows', 'Batch of UP+6xRIGHT just executed - need to verify result']
- Open questions: ['Can frames pass through purple barrier?', 'Does the frame need to be centered in the target?', 'What happens when frame reaches target?']
- Plan: Check if right frame reached target. If yes, select left frame and move it to left target. If not, adjust movement.
- Strategy log: ['Initial exploration: tested RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN on left frame (steps 1-6). Discovered MOUSE selection mechanic (step 7). Moved right frame toward target (steps 8-10).']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 25 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the output, the RIGHT action was executed but the frame position didn't change - it's still at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 (boundary=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]]). Wait, but the UP action was executed before RIGHT. Let me check the ascii output:
Row 30: wwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwg
Row 31: wwwwwNWNppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
Row 32: wwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
The frame is still at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The UP action didn't move it! The frame is still in the same position.
Wait, let me re-read. The previous output showed:
"N frame: boundary=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]], center=(31,31)"
This is the right frame at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. But I executed UP before this print, so it should have moved to rows 27-29. Unless UP didn't work...
Actually wait, I need to check if the UP action actually worked. Let me compare before and after frames. The previous frame (before UP) had the right frame at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. After UP, it should be at rows 27-29, cols 30-32.
But the output shows the frame is still at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. This means UP didn't work on the right frame.
Wait, but the ascii shows:
Row 30: wwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwg
The NNN is at cols 30-32, which means the frame is still at rows 30-32. So UP didn't move the frame.
Hmm, but the board_changed was true. Let me check what changed. Maybe the selection state changed (from charcoal center to white center)?
Actually, looking more carefully at the ascii:
Row 31: wwwwwNWNppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
The center is 'W' (white), which means the frame is now selected. Before, it was 'c' (charcoal). So the MOUSE action changed the center from charcoal to white, and the UP action didn't move the frame.
Wait, but the previous state showed the right frame at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 with a charcoal center. After MOUSE(row=31, col=31), the center became white. Then UP was executed, but the frame didn't move.
So UP doesn't work on the right frame? That's strange. Let me check if there's a different issue.
Actually, looking at the ascii more carefully:
Row 25: ggggggggppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
Row 26: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwg
The right panel (white area) starts at row 26, cols 39-43. The right target is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48.
But the right frame is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32, which is in the LEFT panel (cols 25-29 are white in rows 30-32).
Wait, I'm confused about the layout. Let me re-examine:
- Row 30: wwwwwNNNppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwg
  - cols 25-29: wwwww (white, left panel)
  - cols 30-32: NNN (right frame)
  - cols 33-38: pppppp (purple barrier)
  - cols 39-43: wwwww (white, right panel)
  - cols 44-48: ccccc (right target)
  - cols 49-53: wwwww (white)
  - col 54: g (gray)
So the right frame is at cols 30-32, which is between the left panel and the barrier. The left panel extends from cols 25-29 in row 30, but the frame is at cols 30-32. This means the left panel actually extends to cols 32 (or the frame is on a gray area).
Wait, looking at row 25:
Row 25: ggggggggppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
- cols 25-32: gggggggg (gray)
- cols 33-38: pppppp (purple barrier)
So at row 25, cols 25-32 are gray. But at row 30, cols 25-29 are white (www). This means the left panel starts at some row below 25.
Let me check the full layout:
- The left white panel seems to be at rows 26-32, cols ~5-29 (approximately)
- The right frame is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32, which is just to the right of the left panel and to the left of the barrier
Actually, I think the right frame is in a "corridor" between the left panel and the barrier. The corridor is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32.
Now, the UP action didn't move the frame. This could be because:
1. The frame can't move UP (blocked by something above)
2. The UP action doesn't work on the right frame for some reason
Let me check what's above the frame. Looking at row 27-29:
Row 27: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
Row 28: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
Row 29: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
At cols 30-32 in these rows:
- Row 27, cols 30-32: ggg (gray)
- Row 28, cols 30-32: ggg (gray)
- Row 29, cols 30-32: ggg (gray)
So the area above the frame is gray. Maybe the frame can only move on white areas?
Actually, looking at the layout more carefully, I think the right frame is supposed to move to the RIGHT side of the barrier, but the barrier blocks it. The question is how to get past the barrier.
Wait, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the right frame doesn't need to go to the right target. Maybe the level is already complete once the left frame is in the left target?
But the right target is still empty, and the game is not finished. So both frames need to be in their respective targets.
Let me think about this differently. The right frame is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The right target is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. The barrier is at cols 33-38.
What if the right frame needs to move UP first, then RIGHT, then DOWN to reach the target? But UP didn't work...
Actually, wait. Let me re-check. The UP action was executed, and the board changed (board_changed: true). But the frame position didn't change. What changed?
Let me compare the before and after frames to see what actually changed.
Actually, I think I need to look at the transition. The UP action was executed, and the frame didn't move. But the board changed. What could have changed?
Oh wait, I just realized something. The UP action was executed AFTER the MOUSE action. The MOUSE action changed the center from charcoal to white. So the board_changed could be from the MOUSE action, not the UP action.
But the executed_actions shows both actions were executed. Let me check if the UP action actually moved the frame by looking at the transition.
Actually, I think the issue is that the frame is at the top of its movement range in the left panel. The left panel might only extend from rows 26-32, and the frame is at rows 30-32. Moving UP would put it at rows 27-29, but maybe the panel doesn't extend that far at cols 30-32.
Looking at the ascii:
Row 27: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
Row 28: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
Row 29: ggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwwwg
At cols 30-32 in rows 27-29, it's gray (ggg). So the frame can't move UP because there's no white panel there.
So the right frame is stuck in the corridor at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. It can't move UP (gray area), can't move RIGHT (barrier), and can't move DOWN (probably gray area or panel edge).
Wait, but the frame was originally at rows 30-32, cols 30-32, and I moved it RIGHT multiple times to get here. So it could move RIGHT before. But now it can't move RIGHT because of the barrier.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the right frame is supposed to go to the LEFT target, not the right target? But the left target is already occupied by the left frame.
Or maybe the right frame doesn't need to move at all, and the level is complete when both frames are in their correct positions? But the right frame is not in the right target.
I'm stuck. Let me try a different approach. Let me check if the frame can move LEFT or DOWN.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the problem. The right frame is at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. The right target is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. 
What if the right frame needs to go around the barrier? The barrier is at cols 33-38, rows 26-35 (approximately). If the frame could move DOWN past the barrier, then RIGHT, then UP, it could reach the right target.
Let me try DOWN to see if the frame can move down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after UP+RIGHT
# Frame still at rows 30-32, cols 30-32 - UP didn't move it, RIGHT didn't move it
# Let me check if DOWN works

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
n_frames = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
for n in n_frames:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"N frame: boundary={b}")

# Check ascii around frame
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRows 28-36, cols 25-40:")
for r in range(28, 37):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][25:40]}")

# Try DOWN to see if frame can move down past barrier
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves active green frame right by 3 cols VERIFIED", "LEFT": "Moves active green frame left by 3 cols VERIFIED", "UP": "Moves active green frame up by 3 rows VERIFIED", "DOWN": "Moves active green frame down by 3 rows VERIFIED", "MOUSE": "Clicking on green frame selects it (gives white center) VERIFIED"}, "object_taxonomy": {"g": "gray background", "w": "white panels/squares", "p": "purple vertical bar", "N": "light green 3x3 frames (movable tokens)", "c": "charcoal 5x5 frames (targets)", "W": "white single pixel (selected token center)", "B": "black single pixel (unselected token center)"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "row 63, charcoal strip"}, "win_pattern": {"rule": "Move each green frame into a charcoal target frame"}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left frame in left target. Right frame selected at rows 30-32, cols 30-32. UP and RIGHT both failed (blocked). Barrier at cols 33-38. Right target at rows 26-30, cols 44-48.", "goal_model": "Move right frame to right target, need to find path around barrier", "recent_findings": ["UP blocked for right frame (gray area above)", "RIGHT blocked (barrier)"], "open_questions": ["Can frame move DOWN past barrier?", "Is there a gap in the barrier?"], "current_plan": "Try DOWN to see if frame can move down", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["UP on right frame (blocked)", "RIGHT on right frame (barrier)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
